sources.title: Balloon Dilation of the Eustachian Tube
sources.snippet:
Balloon dilation of the eustachian tube (BDET) is a procedure intended to improve patency by inflating a balloon in the cartilaginous part of the eustachian tube to cause local dilation. ... During the procedure, a saline-filled balloon catheter is introduced into the eustachian tube through the nose using a minimally invasive transnasal endoscopic method.
Pressure is maintained for 2 minutes or less, after which the balloon is emptied and removed. ... During the procedure, a saline-filled balloon catheter is introduced into the eustachian tube through the nose using a minimally invasive transnasal endoscopic method. ... The eustachian tube connects the middle ear space to the nasopharynx.
It ventilates the middle ear space to equalize pressure across the tympanic membrane, clears mucociliary secretions, and protects the middle ear from infection and reflux of nasopharyngeal contents.

sources.title: CG-SURG-117 Balloon Dilation of the Eustachian Tubes
sources.snippet: It is proposed to relieve chronic ear congestion and middle ear and mastoid infections.

Scientific/Clinical Utility Evidence.display_value: No direct evidence linking patent US20150202089A1 ...
Scientific/Clinical Utility Evidence.full_value: No direct evidence linking patent US20150202089A1 to FDA clearance or a 99.7% technical success rate. General evidence for balloon dilation of the Eustachian tube (BDET) includes FDA 510(k) clearances for commercial systems (XprESS® ENT Dilation System in 2017, Next Generation Balloon Dilation System in 2020) and clinical trial data showing efficacy. A 2018 pivotal trial for the AERA system reported ETDQ-7 symptom improvement of 56.2% vs. 8.5% in controls at 6 weeks (p<0.001). A 2025 meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials found BDET significantly increased the chance of achieving a normal Type A tympanogram (relative risk 4.68). Observational studies report long-term symptom reduction in 80% of patients and efficacy in up to 70% of patients. No serious device- or procedure-related adverse events were reported through 52 weeks in RCTs.

comment.original_value: FDA clearance for balloon dilation devices; clinical trials showing 99.7% technical success rate
comment.validator_explanation: The original value makes specific claims (FDA clearance, 99.7% success) for the patented device, but the provided quotes contain no information linking patent US20150202089A1 to those metrics. The quotes confirm FDA clearance for commercial balloon dilation systems as a class and provide general clinical efficacy data from trials and reviews, but these are not attributed to the specific invention in the patent. The validation must distinguish between class-wide evidence and patent-specific evidence.
comment.qc_reasoning: Validator correctly identified that original claims were not patent-specific. Updated value provides accurate class-wide evidence but clarifies lack of patent-specific linkage. This is a significant improvement in accuracy.

sources.snippet: On analysis of 315 participants from three trials who underwent BDET in comparison to control, the relative risk of benefit of post-operatively reaching Type A tympanogram was 4.68 (2.88-7.51) in comparison to control.

sources.title: Eustachian tube dysfunction in adults: from symptoms to treatment
sources.snippet:
**Eustachian tube balloon dilatation**, an innovative treatment introduced over the last few years.
It involves inserting a balloon catheter into your nose ie a thin, inflatable tube.
The balloon catheter is guided to your Eustachian tube where it is inflated and held for several minutes.
It is effective in up to 70% of patients and can be performed under local or general anaesthesia.
It is a quick day case procedure, with minimal complications, and you can return to work the next day.

(or Acclarent Balloon Inflation Device) and Relieva Extension Tubing.

Inflation Device (or Acclarent Balloon Inflation Device) is used to inflate the ETBC.

Acclarent SE Inflation Device and Acclarent Balloon Inflation Device are FDA cleared devices
(K150172 and K052198, respectively).

The non-compliant balloon is designed to
dilate the cartilaginous portion of the Eustachian tube (ET).
The shaft consists of dual lumen
tubing with an actuator component that is designed to assist in rotation and advancement of the ... Durability of treatment effect is supported by sustained outcomes for over 170 subjects, where
62.2% (61/98) randomized ETBC and 58.1% (43/74) lead-in subjects demonstrated
tympanogram normalization at 24 weeks post-procedure compared to 51.8% (73/141) and 40.0%
(30/75) at 6 weeks, respectively.
Durability was consistently demonstrated across efficacy
outcome measures where improvement in ETDQ-7 scores, proportion of subjects achieving a
MID level improvement, and proportion of subjects without symptomatic dysfunction are
sustained at 24 weeks post-procedure." (https://www.accessdata.fda.gov/cdrh_docs/reviews/den150056.pdf)

sources.title: Acclarent Announces U.S. Launch of ACCLARENT AERA ...
sources.snippet:
“Health care providers treating ETD frequently see ear infections, pressure or blockage, or complaints such as hearing loss and pain,” said Dr. Dennis S.
Poe, professor of otolaryngology at Harvard Medical School and Boston Children’s Hospital. “As discussed at the recent American Academy of Otolaryngology annual meeting, doctors do their best to treat these conditions, but until now – with the launch of ACCLARENT AERA™ – options did not address the root of the problem with the Eustachian tube itself.” ... ACCLARENT AERA™ leverages the company’s innovative balloon catheter technology to dilate dysfunctional Eustachian tubes by inserting a small balloon into the tube and inflating the balloon.

sources.title: Acclarent Announces U.S. Launch of ACCLARENT AERA ...
sources.snippet:
“Health care providers treating ETD frequently see ear infections, pressure or blockage, or complaints such as hearing loss and pain,” said Dr. Dennis S.
Poe, professor of otolaryngology at Harvard Medical School and Boston Children’s Hospital. “As discussed at the recent American Academy of Otolaryngology annual meeting, doctors do their best to treat these conditions, but until now – with the launch of ACCLARENT AERA™ – options did not address the root of the problem with the Eustachian tube itself.” ... ACCLARENT AERA™ leverages the company’s innovative balloon catheter technology to dilate dysfunctional Eustachian tubes by inserting a small balloon into the tube and inflating the balloon.

Using a 20 x 3 mm balloon catheter inflated to 12 ATM to perform three consecutive dilations at different depths within the ET, 120 seconds for each dilation appears to be an adequate time to obtain a good dilation without risking mucosal ischaemia.

sources.title: How do heat packs work? | Therapeutic Pillow Blogpillows.com.au › blog › ufaq › how-do-heat-packs-work
sources.snippet:
Some heat packs work by utilizing a chemical reaction to generate heat.
These packs typically contain a mixture of ingredients such as iron powder, salt, and activated carbon.
When the pack is exposed to air or shaken, the iron powder oxidizes, releasing heat as a byproduct.
This process is known as an exothermic reaction.
The salt and activated carbon help to speed up the reaction and enhance heat production.
Once activated, the heat pack can provide warmth for a specific duration, usually several hours, making it a convenient and portable solution for keeping warm in cold weather or soothing sore muscles.

Scientific/Clinical Utility Evidence.display_value: No clinical trial results, FDA clearances, or prod...
Scientific/Clinical Utility Evidence.full_value: No clinical trial results, FDA clearances, or product launches found for US4408605A. The patent describes a theoretical mechanism: heat from an exothermic chemical reaction is intended to warm air in the middle ear, increasing its volume and reducing pressure differentials to relieve pain from eardrum deflection. The device is described as inexpensive and intended for use by non-medically trained persons.

comment.original_value: Heat increases air volume in middle ear, reduces painful eardrum deflection
comment.validator_explanation: Searches for the patent number and associated terms found only the patent document itself. No evidence of clinical studies, FDA clearance, or commercial product launch was found in the provided sources. The current value is a simplified description of the patent's claimed mechanism, not evidence of utility.
comment.qc_reasoning: Corrected from speculative mechanism description to accurate statement of absence of clinical evidence. The original value presented theoretical mechanism as evidence, while the updated value correctly states no clinical evidence exists.

The device is suitable for treatment of both small children and adults. - secretory otitis media with effusion which leads to negative pressure and to the formation of liquid in the middle ear, causing hearing loss to those suffering therefrom." (https://patents.google.com/patent/EP2629723A1/en)

Primary Function/Mechanism.display_value: Pressure equalization tube (tympanostomy tube) wit...
Primary Function/Mechanism.full_value: Pressure equalization tube (tympanostomy tube) with asymmetric flanges

comment.original_value: Pressure equalization tube with asymmetric flanges
comment.validator_explanation: The current value is accurate and confirmed by the patent document. The patent describes a tympanic membrane pressure equalization tube configured as a tubular body with asymmetric flanges [1]. This is a standard surgical device for middle ear ventilation, also known as a tympanostomy tube, grommet, or ventilation tube [2].
comment.key_citation: [V1] Tympanic Membrane Pressure Equalization Tube (0.95): "- the invention is directed to a tympanic membrane pressure equalization tube system comprising a tympanic membrane pressure equalization tube and an introducer. - the tympanic membrane pressure equalization tube comprises a tubular body with a distal end and a proximal end and a lumen therebetween, a medial flange located at the distal end of the tubular body, said medial flange comprising two or more retention elements and a space between each retention element. ... - the tympanic membrane pressure equalization tube according to the invention is a grommet like device which is folded and/or compressed within the tube, and recovers its shape when delivered into the tympanic membrane. ... - the tube 100 is configured as a tubular body with asymmetric flanges, a medial flange 102 a lateral flange 104 and a tube lumen 106 ." (https://patents.google.com/patent/US20150196430A1/en)

sources.title: Tympanic Membrane Pressure Equalization Tube
sources.snippet: - the invention is directed to a tympanic membrane pressure equalization tube system comprising a tympanic membrane pressure equalization tube and an introducer. - the tympanic membrane pressure equalization tube comprises a tubular body with a distal end and a proximal end and a lumen therebetween, a medial flange located at the distal end of the tubular body, said medial flange comprising two or more retention elements and a space between each retention element. ... - the tympanic membrane pressure equalization tube according to the invention is a grommet like device which is folded and/or compressed within the tube, and recovers its shape when delivered into the tympanic membrane. ... - the tube 100 is configured as a tubular body with asymmetric flanges, a medial flange 102 a lateral flange 104 and a tube lumen 106 .

sources.title: Table 2. Definition Of...
sources.snippet:
Other names for tympanostomy tubes are grommet, myringotomy tube, or pressure equalizing tube. ... The surgeon makes a small slit in the eardrum and drains the fluid in the middle ear cleft [4].
In cases where the incision doesn't drain the fluid completely, or there is a recurrent infection, a small tube is placed through the slit. ... It drains the fluid that collects and allows air into the middle ear to keep it ventilated.
Tympanostomy tubes are placed to maintain tympanic membrane patency so that the middle ear can drain and the negative middle ear pressure is relieved. ... Tympanostomy tubes help drain middle ear fluid and provide a ventilatory port to the middle ear cavity [9]. In cases of acute otitis media that is refractory to antibiotic therapy, a tympanostomy tube is indicated.
Tympanostomy tubes can also be used for patients who cannot tolerate oral antibiotics.
In these cases, tympanostomy drains not only the middle ear fluid but also allows easy access to topical antibiotic drops to the middle ear cavity.
Also, in cases of otitis media with effusion where hearing is impaired because of fluid accumulation, a tympanostomy tube is indicated.
Complications of otitis media such as facial nerve palsy, otomastoiditis, and meningitis are additional indications for tympanostomy tubes. ... In these cases, tympanostomy drains not only the middle ear fluid but also allows easy access to topical antibiotic drops to the middle ear cavity. ... Tympanostomy tubes should be inserted as soon as possible to ventilate the middle ear area and stop the eardrum from retracting further under negative pressure. ... The main indication for the use of this tube is eustachian tube dysfunction leading to otitis media with effusion or serous otitis media.

sources.title: Ear tubes - Mayo Clinic
sources.snippet:
- **Middle ear infection, known as acute otitis media.** Bacteria or viruses cause this infection.
It causes inflammation and fluids in the middle ear.
Ear tubes might help prevent new infections.
Children who have three or more infections in six months or four or more infections in a year might be helped from ear tubes.
- **Buildup of fluids without infection, also known as otitis media with effusion.** One cause of this is fluid that stays in the ear after an infection.
Other causes include problems with the eustachian tubes or another condition that keeps fluid from draining.
Fluid buildup can cause hearing loss and balance problems.
Ear tubes might help with hearing problems that cause a delay in speaking or other learning delays.
These delays can cause problems in school.
- **Ongoing middle ear infection, also known as chronic middle ear infection.** This infection, which is caused by bacteria, doesn't get better with antibiotics.
An ear tube can drain the ear and make a way for antibiotic drops to be put right into the middle ear.
- **Ongoing inflammation of the middle ear, also known as chronic suppurative otitis media.** This inflammation causes a tear in the eardrum and continuing drainage from the ear.
An infection, a blocked eustachian tube or injury to the ear can cause the tear.
An ear tube can help the ear drain after surgery to fix the eardrum.
The tube also makes a way for ear drops to be put right into the middle ear.

sources.title: Ear Tubes (Tympanostomy): Definition & Procedure
sources.snippet: In adults, ear tubes are most commonly used to treat barotrauma — a painful condition caused by air pressure changes.

Detailed Working Description.display_value: A small, hollow tube is surgically inserted throug...
Detailed Working Description.full_value: A small, hollow tube is surgically inserted through an incision (myringotomy) in the tympanic membrane. The tubular body spans the membrane, with a medial (inner) flange and a lateral (outer) flange to secure it in place. The central lumen provides a continuous airway, allowing fluid to drain from the middle ear to the outer ear canal and permitting air entry to equalize pressure and ventilate the middle ear space [2]. The tube is designed to be folded/compressed during delivery and recovers its shape when deployed [1].

comment.original_value: Tubular body with medial and lateral flanges; lumen for fluid communication between middle and outer ear
comment.validator_explanation: The current value is correct but brief. The synthesis from high-confidence sources provides a more detailed, step-by-step explanation. The procedure involves creating a slit (myringotomy), suctioning fluid, and placing the tube [2]. The flanges anchor the tube on either side of the eardrum, and the lumen enables its dual function of drainage and ventilation [2]. The patent also describes a delivery mechanism where the tube is compressed and then expands into place [1].

Intended Treatment(s).display_value: Otitis media (acute and chronic), otitis media wit...
Intended Treatment(s).full_value: Otitis media (acute and chronic), otitis media with effusion (serous otitis media), recurrent ear infections, Eustachian tube dysfunction, and associated complications (e.g., tympanic membrane retraction, barotitis media) [2]. Also used for patients requiring hyperbaric oxygen therapy or as a conduit for topical medication delivery [3].

comment.original_value: Otitis media, otitis media with effusion, recurrent ear infections
comment.validator_explanation: The current value lists key conditions but is not exhaustive. High-confidence sources confirm treatment for acute, chronic, and effusive otitis media [4]. They also expand indications to include Eustachian tube dysfunction (the main underlying cause) [2], prevention of complications like retraction pockets [2], use in barotrauma [5], and for administering topical medications [2].

Key Claims.display_value: 1. A tympanic membrane pressure equalization tube ...
Key Claims.full_value: 1. A tympanic membrane pressure equalization tube comprising a tubular body with a distal end, a proximal end, a lumen therebetween, a medial flange at the distal end, and a lateral flange at the proximal end, wherein the flanges are asymmetric [1]. 2. The medial flange comprises two or more retention elements with spaces between them [1]. 3. The tube is part of a system that includes an introducer for delivery [1]. 4. The tube is configured to be folded and/or compressed within a delivery shield and to recover its shape when deployed into the tympanic membrane [1].

comment.original_value: Asymmetric flanges, retention elements, specific diameter specifications
comment.validator_explanation: The current value was vague. Direct access to the patent document (US20150196430A1, which appears to be a related or continuation application) provides specific, high-confidence claims. The core patented features are the asymmetric flanges, the specific design of the medial flange with retention elements, and the integration with a delivery system that allows for compressed deployment and shape recovery.

sources.title: Ear tubes - Mayo Clinic
sources.snippet:
- **Middle ear infection, known as acute otitis media.** Bacteria or viruses cause this infection.
It causes inflammation and fluids in the middle ear.
Ear tubes might help prevent new infections.
Children who have three or more infections in six months or four or more infections in a year might be helped from ear tubes.
- **Buildup of fluids without infection, also known as otitis media with effusion.** One cause of this is fluid that stays in the ear after an infection.
Other causes include problems with the eustachian tubes or another condition that keeps fluid from draining.
Fluid buildup can cause hearing loss and balance problems.
Ear tubes might help with hearing problems that cause a delay in speaking or other learning delays.
These delays can cause problems in school.
- **Ongoing middle ear infection, also known as chronic middle ear infection.** This infection, which is caused by bacteria, doesn't get better with antibiotics.
An ear tube can drain the ear and make a way for antibiotic drops to be put right into the middle ear.
- **Ongoing inflammation of the middle ear, also known as chronic suppurative otitis media.** This inflammation causes a tear in the eardrum and continuing drainage from the ear.
An infection, a blocked eustachian tube or injury to the ear can cause the tear.
An ear tube can help the ear drain after surgery to fix the eardrum.
The tube also makes a way for ear drops to be put right into the middle ear.

sources.title: Ear Tubes (Tympanostomy): Definition & Procedure
sources.snippet: In adults, ear tubes are most commonly used to treat barotrauma — a painful condition caused by air pressure changes.

Scientific/Clinical Utility Evidence.display_value: Tympanostomy tube placement is the most frequently...
Scientific/Clinical Utility Evidence.full_value: Tympanostomy tube placement is the most frequently performed ambulatory surgery in children in the US [ncbi_tympanostomy-tube-placement_clinical-significance, S1.1.59-p0.85]. Clinical studies show the procedure improves quality of life in children [ncbi_tympanostomy-tube-placement_quality-of-life, S1.1.60-p0.85]. In patients with chronic otitis media with effusion (OME), tympanostomy tube insertion reduces the prevalence of middle ear effusion by 33% and improves average hearing by 5 to 12 decibels [ncbi_tympanostomy-tube-placement_quality-of-life, S1.1.60-p0.85]. The procedure is a standard surgical intervention for recurrent acute otitis media, chronic OME, and persistent middle ear infections [ncbi_tympanostomy-tube-insertion_indications, S1.1.15-p0.85].

comment.original_value: More than 1 million tympanostomy tubes placed annually; standard surgical procedure
comment.validator_explanation: The original value correctly states it's a standard procedure but lacks specific clinical evidence. The updated answer provides authoritative clinical efficacy data from NIH sources, including quantitative hearing improvement and prevalence reduction.
comment.key_citation: [V1] Anatomy and Physiology (0.85): "Tympanostomy tube insertion has been shown to improve the quality of life in children.[17] In patients with chronic OME, the prevalence of middle ear effusion is reduced by 33%, and average hearing is improved by 5 to 12 decibels.[20] Tympanostomy tube placement allows topical drops to be administered directly into the middle ear at higher concentrations and limits the use of systemic antibiotics in children.[4] ... Tympanostomy tube insertion is primarily indicated for conditions such as recurrent acute otitis media (AOM), chronic otitis media with effusion (OME), and persistent middle ear infections that fail to respond to conservative management." (https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK565858/)

Primary Function/Mechanism.display_value: Automated delivery system for pressure equalizatio...
Primary Function/Mechanism.full_value: Automated delivery system for pressure equalization tubes

comment.original_value: Automated delivery system for pressure equalization tubes
comment.validator_explanation: Patent claims confirm 'systems and methods for automatically puncturing and delivering a tympanic membrane equalization tube' [1]
comment.key_citation:
[V1] Tympanic membrane pressure equalization tube delivery system (0.95): "- the present invention provides systems and methods for automatically puncturing and delivering a tympanic membrane equalization tube (i.e., tympanostomy tube) into a tympanic membrane. ... - a system for delivering a pressure equalization tube.
- the system includes a housing including a handle. ... - An elongate shaft assembly is coupled with the housing.
- the shaft assembly includes an outer shaft having a blunt (atraumatic) distal tip portion. ... - the third cam follower is attached to the shield. ... - the fourth cam follower is attached to the dilator. - a spring may be biased between the housing and the cam shaft.
- the spring has a wound position, which places torsion on the cam shaft, and a released position. ... - a release button may be moveably coupled to the cam shaft.
- the release button has a first position, which maintains the spring in the wound position, and a second position, which allows the spring to move into the released position.
- the release button is moved into the released position, the spring is released to move the cam shaft and cause the cam followers to linearly move respective portions of the shaft assembly to form an incision in a tympanic membrane using the cutting member, dilate the incision using the dilator, and advance the pressure equalization tube out of the shield and into the incision using the pusher. ... - the shield is retracted into the shaft, thereby releasing a distal flange of the pressure equalization tube such that it assumes an expanded configuration.
- the pressure equalization tube is pushed out of the shield using a pusher disposed within the shield, thereby releasing a proximal flange of the pressure equalization tube such that it assumes an expanded configuration." (https://patents.google.com/patent/US20110015645)

Scientific/Clinical Utility Evidence.full_value: No FDA clearance, product launch, or published clinical trial results found specifically for patent US20110015645A1. The original claim 'More accurate and consistent PE tube deployment; minimal trauma' appears to be a description of intended benefits from the patent application, not documented clinical evidence. However, related commercial systems (e.g., Hummingbird TTS) have FDA 510(k) clearance [1] and associated clinical trials (NCT01046877, NCT02165384) evaluating procedural success of tympanostomy tube delivery systems [2][3].

sources.title: System and Method for the Simultaneous Bilateral Placement of Pressure Equalization Tubes
sources.snippet: - the present invention provides systems and methods for the provision of painless, simultaneous, bilateral treatment of the target tissues of the ear of children suffering from recurrent otitis or otitis media with effusion in an office-based procedure with awake, mobile patients, created to manage the child's anxiety. - the present invention provides systems and methods for bilateral treatment of a patient having a head with a first ear and a second ear that function by aligning a first device with a target tissue of the first ear; aligning a second device with a target tissue of the second ear; and therapeutically remodeling, with synchronization, the target tissues of the first and second ear with the first and second aligned devices, respectively. - the present invention provides systems and methods for the direct visualization and the targeting and range finding for the target tissue of the ear which facilitate the visual and/or optical monitoring and thus a more effective treatment of the target tissue region. ... - such systems and methods facilitate performing treatment procedures such as myringotomy, tympanostomy tube placement, tympanocentisis and the like, under local (rather than general) anesthesia, often in a doctor's office (rather than an out-patient surgical facility). ... - the embodiments of the present invention provide systems and methods for the provision of painless, simultaneous, bilateral treatment of the target tissues of the ear of children suffering from recurrent otitis or otitis media with effusion in an office-based procedure in awake, mobile patients, designed to manage the child's and the parent's anxiety. - the present invention is directed toward a suite of medical devices that shares a common support structure that is used to align various therapeutic devices with the target tissues of the ear of children.

comment.original_value: Pressure-controlled air delivery device (EarPopper)
comment.validator_explanation: The current value accurately describes the core operating principle. The EarPopper is a pressure-controlled device that delivers regulated air flow [1]. It is based on the Politzer Maneuver, a 120-year-old proven medical procedure called Politzerization [2]. The device delivers a safe, constant, regulated flow of air into the nasal cavity [1]. This is consistent with the patent US8197552B2 which describes a Eustachian Tube Device and Method. The value is concise, accurate, and appropriately describes the primary function as a pressure-controlled air delivery system specifically designed for the EarPopper device.

Based on the Politzer Maneuver, the EarPopper
delivers a safe, constant, regulated flow of air into the nasal cavity. ... During the moment of swallowing the air is diverted up the eustachian tube, clearing and ventilating the middle ear. The EarPopper relieves negative ear pressure and allows any accumulated fluids to drain. ®" (https://summitmedicalusa.com/otology/earpopper-middle-ear-inflation-device/)

Detailed Working Description.display_value: Delivers constant volume velocity of air through n...
Detailed Working Description.full_value: Delivers constant volume velocity of air through nose to Eustachian tube orifice; enlarges torus tubarius aperture temporarily. During swallowing, air is diverted up the Eustachian tube, clearing and ventilating the middle ear.

comment.original_value: Delivers constant volume velocity of air through nose to Eustachian tube orifice; enlarges torus tubarius aperture temporarily
comment.validator_explanation: The current value is accurate but can be enhanced with additional mechanistic details. The EarPopper delivers a safe, constant, regulated flow of air into the nasal cavity [1]. The key mechanism is that during the moment of swallowing, the air is diverted up the Eustachian tube, clearing and ventilating the middle ear [1]. The device relieves negative ear pressure and allows any accumulated fluids to drain [1]. The treatment only takes a few seconds and is safe for children and adults [2]. The updated value adds the critical swallowing mechanism that was missing from the original description, providing a more complete step-by-step explanation of how the device works.

Specific conditions treated include: Otitis Media with Effusion [1], Negative Ear Pressure caused by rapid elevation changes [1], and Eustachian Tube Dysfunction [1]. Scientific/Clinical Utility Evidence.display_value: FDA 510(k) clearance K073401 for EarPopper device;...
Scientific/Clinical Utility Evidence.full_value: FDA 510(k) clearance K073401 for EarPopper device; indicated for treatment of negative middle ear pressure, otitis media with effusion, and Eustachian tube dysfunction [1]. Clinical studies funded by NIH (Grant#: 5R44DC003613-03) showed effectiveness in reducing chronic middle ear effusions [1]. A 2022 prospective interventional study (n=32) showed improvement in 81.3% of otitis media with effusion patients using the EarPopper scoring system [2]. A randomized controlled trial protocol (IRB Approval Number: 18-0388) evaluates efficacy in reducing acute otitis media episodes in children with recurrent otitis media [ncbi_earpopper-study-protocol_irb-approval, S1.1.19-p0.85]. Device delivers air at 5.2 PSI, 1,524 mL/min [ncbi_earpopper-device_air-pressure, S1.1.18-p0.85].

Scientific/Clinical Utility Evidence.display_value: Patent US20030208250A1 describes radiofrequency en...
Scientific/Clinical Utility Evidence.full_value: Patent US20030208250A1 describes radiofrequency energy application to Eustachian tube tissues, but no clinical trials, FDA clearances, product launches, or published efficacy data were found for this specific patent. The current value 'Effective in treating Eustachian tube dysfunction by enhancing structural integrity' appears to be a general description of the intended mechanism rather than documented clinical evidence. Extensive searches found no clinical trials registered under this patent number, no FDA 510(k) clearances associated with it, and no marketed products. The patent itself expired without apparent commercialization [1]. While balloon dilation for Eustachian tube dysfunction has been clinically validated through multiple studies showing effectiveness (e.g., AERA system with 99.7% technical success rate [2], tympanogram normalization rates of 51.8% vs 13.9% for controls [3], and 80% long-term symptom reduction [4]), these studies relate to balloon dilation devices, not radiofrequency energy application. The radiofrequency approach described in US20030208250A1 represents a different mechanism targeting specific muscles (tensor veli palatini, salpinopharyngeal, levator veli palatini) rather than mechanical dilation [5]. No evidence was found that this radiofrequency approach progressed to clinical testing or commercialization.

comment.original_value: Effective in treating Eustachian tube dysfunction by enhancing structural integrity
comment.validator_explanation: Original value lacks documented clinical evidence; it appears to be a theoretical description of mechanism rather than validated utility data. No clinical trials, FDA clearances, or product launches found for this specific patent's radiofrequency approach, despite extensive searches of ClinicalTrials.gov, FDA databases, and medical literature. The patent expired without apparent commercialization.
comment.qc_reasoning: Validator provided comprehensive evidence of absence - patent expired without commercialization, no clinical evidence found for this specific approach

**Patent number:**11850120 **Abstract:**An apparatus comprises a shaft, an expandable dilator, and at least one ventilation pathway.
The shaft defines a longitudinal axis and comprises a distal and proximal ends with at least one shaft lumen.
The expandable dilator comprises body with its own proximal and distal ends.
The body is configured to transition between a contracted state and an expanded state.
The body is configured to dilate a Eustachian tube of a patient in the expanded state.
The at least one ventilation pathway is configured to provide ventilation from the distal end of the body to the proximal end of the body when the body is in the expanded state.
In some examples, the ventilation pathway comprises a set of transversely oriented vent openings formed through the shaft.

Scientific/Clinical Utility Evidence.display_value: FDA 510(k) clearance granted for multiple Eustachi...
Scientific/Clinical Utility Evidence.full_value: FDA 510(k) clearance granted for multiple Eustachian tube balloon dilation systems: XprESS ENT Dilation System (2017, adults 18+) [1], Next Generation Balloon Dilation System (2020, adults 18+) [1], and Acclarent AERA Eustachian Tube Balloon Dilation System (2023, pediatric ages 8-17) [2]. Clinical evidence from pivotal trial by Poe et al. (2018) demonstrated ETDQ-7 symptom improvement significantly greater in BDET group vs. controls at 6 weeks (56.2% vs. 8.5%, p<0.001) [1]. Long-term effectiveness shown with balloon dilation providing up to 29 months of relief [3]. Retrospective evaluation at Turku University Hospital (2013-2016, n=107 patients, 167 ears) found 80% (36/45) reported long-term reduction of symptoms with mean follow-up of 33 months [4]. Symptoms significantly improved included aural fullness, otalgia, recurrent acute otitis media, otitis media with effusion, and ability to perform Valsalva maneuver [4]. All procedures performed without complications in multiple studies [5]. Commercial products include Stryker Audion Eustachian Tube dilation system with FlexFeel technology [3] and Acclarent AERA system (first FDA-cleared for pediatric use) [2]. Clinical utility established for treating chronic obstructive ETD in adults with symptoms lasting 12+ months [6], and approximately 1.48 million U.S. adolescents suffer from OETD [2].

comment.original_value: FDA clearance for balloon dilation; clinical utility demonstrated
comment.validator_explanation: Original value was accurate but lacked specificity. Updated with comprehensive FDA clearance details (specific systems, years, age groups), quantitative clinical trial results (ETDQ-7 improvement 56.2% vs 8.5%, p<0.001), long-term efficacy data (29 months relief, 80% symptom reduction at 33 months follow-up), safety profile (no complications), and commercial product names (Acclarent AERA, Stryker Audion, XprESS ENT). High confidence based on multiple authoritative sources including FDA clearances, peer-reviewed clinical studies, and manufacturer documentation.

sources.title: Balloon Dilation of the Eustachian Tube
sources.snippet:
Balloon dilation of the eustachian tube (BDET) for treatment of chronic obstructive eustachian tube dysfunction (ETD) is considered
**MEDICALLY NECESSARY** under the following conditions:
- Adults (age 22 years and older) with symptoms of obstructive ETD (aural fullness, aural pressure, otalgia, and/or hearing loss) for 12 months or longer in 1 or both ears that significantly affects quality of life or functional health status;
- Aural fullness and pressure must be present (see Policy Guidelines).

Scientific/Clinical Utility Evidence.display_value: Clinical studies demonstrate safety and efficacy o...
Scientific/Clinical Utility Evidence.full_value: Clinical studies demonstrate safety and efficacy of balloon dilation for Eustachian tube dysfunction (ETD). A 2022 retrospective study of 107 patients (167 ears) found 80% reported long-term symptom reduction at a mean follow-up of 33 months, with significant improvements in aural fullness, otalgia, recurrent acute otitis media, and ability to perform Valsalva maneuver [1]. A 2021 real-world study of 169 participants found 85.1% achieved the minimum clinically important difference in symptom scores at 6 months, with 50.6% achieving normalized scores [2]. The procedure is considered safe, with studies reporting no serious perioperative complications and mild side effects [1]. Success rates for symptom improvement range from 70-87% across studies [3].

Primary Function/Mechanism.display_value: Gene therapy delivery device for intracochlear adm...
Primary Function/Mechanism.full_value: Gene therapy delivery device for intracochlear administration of AAV-vectored gene therapy

comment.original_value: Gene therapy delivery device
comment.validator_explanation: The current value 'Gene therapy delivery device' is accurate but incomplete. The device is specifically designed for intracochlear delivery of AAVAnc80-hOTOF gene therapy [1]. The primary function is to enable precise, atraumatic delivery of viral vectors containing the OTOF gene into the cochlear perilymphatic space through the round window membrane [2]. The device includes both an Akouos Delivery Device and a Precision Delivery Mechanism that work together to facilitate controlled injection into the intracochlear space [1]. This is fundamentally different from traditional ear pressure equalization devices mentioned in the general notes - it is a specialized surgical instrument for gene therapy administration, not a pressure management device. The device enables endoscopic access where the eardrum is partially lifted to allow transient insertion into the round window, a tiny entry point into the cochlea [2]. Original confidence rated Medium as it captured the general category but lacked technical specificity.

On October 4, 2023, the patient underwent a surgical procedure at CHOP where the gene therapy was placed into the inner ear using a device called an endoscope that allows the ear drum to be partially lifted, allowing an investigational medical device to be transiently inserted into the “round window,” a tiny entry point into the cochlea.
A single, small dose of a gene therapy (AK-OTOF) containing copies of the normal *OTOF *gene was then delivered directly to the inner ear. ... Functioning *OTOF* genes are encased in a viral vector, a modified form of a non-disease-causing virus, which allows them to be delivered into cells of the cochlea in the inner ear.
The vector solution is directly injected into the internal fluid of the cochlea during the procedure.

sources.title: Cochlear Gene Therapy - PMC - PubMed Central
sources.snippet:
The procedure involves a post-auricular, trans-bulla approach to the cochlea.
The round window can be punctured with a glass-pulled micropipette for gene delivery, or a 25-gauge needle can be used to perform a cochleostomy.
Multiple studies over many years have documented that the approach is safe, relatively easy to perform, and poses little risk to residual/native hearing (Praetorius et al. 2007; Kawamoto et al. 2009; Akil et al. 2015).

sources.title: Advances in cochlear gene therapies - PMC
sources.snippet:
Adeno-associated virus (AAV) has emerged as a preferred vector for gene delivery to the inner ear due to broad cellular tropism, low immunogenicity, inability to establish productive infection in the absence of a helper virus, and well-documented non-ototoxicity [31, 40].
AAV2/2, AAV2/9, and the synthetic AAV2/Anc80L65 and AAV2/9PHP.eB exhibit highly efficient transduction of cochlear hair cells and are now preferred by many groups [31, 33, 38, 41, 42]. ... Two precision medicine pharmaceutical developers, Akouos and Decibel Therapeutics, recently began phase 1/2 clinical trials of gene therapies for *OTOF*-related hearing loss [66, 67]. *OTOF* encodes otoferlin, a calcium sensor required for exocytosis of synaptic vesicles at the basolateral membrane of inner hair cells; homozygous loss-of-function results in auditory neuropathy spectrum disorder presenting with congenital severe-to-profound hearing loss [68].

sources.title: gene therapy offers hope for OTOF-mediated hearing loss ...
sources.snippet: Among these genetic causes, an OTOF gene mutation is responsible for deafness in roughly 200 000 individuals globally. Specifically, the mutation results in autosomal recessive deafness 9 (DFNB9), which contributes to 2–8% of all cases of congenital genetic deafness^3,4^. ... Otoferlin plays a crucial role in facilitating the transmission of these signals by interacting with calcium, SNAP25, and Synataxin‐1 to facilitate the exocytotic release of glutamate, an excitatory neurotransmitter. ... To address this limitation, dual-AAV delivery strategy of splitting the OTOF gene into two and inserting them into two AAV vectors was utilized^12^.

sources.title: Otoferlin gene therapy restores hearing in deaf children
sources.snippet: Over 220 pathogenic variants in OTOF have been identified, and most commonly result in recessively inherited, congenital or prelingual, severe to profound hearing loss known as DNFB9.^4^ The observation that the majority of inner hair cells, outer hair cells, and spiral ganglion neurons persist after birth in OTOF null mouse mutants suggested that *Otof* gene replacement in inner hair cells may be a viable strategy to restore hearing.^5^^,^^6^ Dual-rAAV vector approaches were developed that bypassed the constraints on rAAV packaging capacity, recapitulated the full-length *Otof* cDNA in inner hair cells, generated *Otof* mRNA and protein, and rescued auditory function in OTOF null mice.

Detailed Working Description.display_value: The device enables surgical delivery of AAVAnc80-h...
Detailed Working Description.full_value: The device enables surgical delivery of AAVAnc80-hOTOF gene therapy through a multi-step procedure: (1) A post-auricular, trans-bulla surgical approach accesses the middle ear; (2) An endoscope allows the tympanic membrane (eardrum) to be partially lifted; (3) The investigational medical device is transiently inserted through the round window membrane, a tiny natural entry point into the cochlea; (4) A single, controlled dose of AAVAnc80-hOTOF viral vector solution (containing functional OTOF genes encased in adeno-associated virus) is directly injected into the perilymphatic fluid of the cochlea; (5) The device includes a Precision Delivery Mechanism to ensure accurate placement and minimize trauma to delicate cochlear structures; (6) Performance is monitored based on frequency of device deficiencies during delivery. The viral vectors then transduce inner hair cells, delivering functional OTOF gene copies to restore otoferlin protein production and enable synaptic transmission.

comment.original_value: Intracochlear administration of AAVAnc80-hOTOF using a sterile, one-time use investigational medical device
comment.validator_explanation: The current value provides basic information but lacks the comprehensive step-by-step detail required. The complete working description integrates multiple high-quality sources: The surgical approach involves a post-auricular, trans-bulla approach to access the cochlea [3]. The procedure uses an endoscope to partially lift the eardrum, allowing transient insertion of the investigational device into the round window [2]. The device delivers a single dose of gene therapy containing functional OTOF genes encased in a viral vector (modified non-disease-causing virus) directly into the internal fluid of the cochlea [2]. The system includes both an Akouos Delivery Device and Precision Delivery Mechanism working together [1]. Device performance is assessed based on frequency of deficiencies observed during delivery to the intracochlear space [4]. The viral vectors (AAVAnc80) efficiently transduce cochlear hair cells with low immunogenicity and documented non-ototoxicity [5]. This is a sterile, one-time use device [1]. Original confidence Medium as it captured key elements but lacked procedural detail.

sources.title: Otoferlin gene therapy restores hearing in deaf children
sources.snippet: Over 220 pathogenic variants in OTOF have been identified, and most commonly result in recessively inherited, congenital or prelingual, severe to profound hearing loss known as DNFB9.^4^ The observation that the majority of inner hair cells, outer hair cells, and spiral ganglion neurons persist after birth in OTOF null mouse mutants suggested that *Otof* gene replacement in inner hair cells may be a viable strategy to restore hearing.^5^^,^^6^ Dual-rAAV vector approaches were developed that bypassed the constraints on rAAV packaging capacity, recapitulated the full-length *Otof* cDNA in inner hair cells, generated *Otof* mRNA and protein, and rescued auditory function in OTOF null mice.

Intended Treatment(s).display_value: Profound bilateral sensorineural hearing loss due ...
Intended Treatment(s).full_value: Profound bilateral sensorineural hearing loss due to biallelic pathogenic mutations in the otoferlin (OTOF) gene, also known as autosomal recessive deafness 9 (DFNB9) or OTOF-mediated auditory neuropathy spectrum disorder

comment.original_value: Profound bilateral sensorineural hearing loss due to otoferlin gene mutations
comment.validator_explanation: The current value is accurate and well-specified. The device treats profound bilateral sensorineural hearing loss caused by at least two mutations in the otoferlin gene [1]. This condition is formally known as DFNB9 (autosomal recessive deafness 9), which contributes to 2-8% of all congenital genetic deafness cases and affects approximately 200,000 individuals globally [6]. Over 220 pathogenic variants in OTOF have been identified, most commonly resulting in congenital or prelingual severe to profound hearing loss [7]. The OTOF gene encodes otoferlin, a calcium sensor required for exocytosis of synaptic vesicles at the basolateral membrane of inner hair cells; homozygous loss-of-function results in auditory neuropathy spectrum disorder [5]. Inclusion criteria specify clinical presentation of profound bilateral sensorineural hearing loss as assessed by ABR (Auditory Brainstem Response) [4]. The treatment is specifically designed for patients without bilateral cochlear implants [4]. Original confidence High as the value was precise and complete.

sources.title: Children's Hospital of Philadelphia Performs First in U.S. Gene ...
sources.snippet:
On October 4, 2023, the patient underwent a surgical procedure at CHOP where the gene therapy was placed into the inner ear using a device called an endoscope that allows the ear drum to be partially lifted, allowing an investigational medical device to be transiently inserted into the “round window,” a tiny entry point into the cochlea.
A single, small dose of a gene therapy (AK-OTOF) containing copies of the normal *OTOF *gene was then delivered directly to the inner ear. ... Functioning *OTOF* genes are encased in a viral vector, a modified form of a non-disease-causing virus, which allows them to be delivered into cells of the cochlea in the inner ear.
The vector solution is directly injected into the internal fluid of the cochlea during the procedure.

sources.title: Cochlear Gene Therapy - PMC - PubMed Central
sources.snippet:
The procedure involves a post-auricular, trans-bulla approach to the cochlea.
The round window can be punctured with a glass-pulled micropipette for gene delivery, or a 25-gauge needle can be used to perform a cochleostomy.
Multiple studies over many years have documented that the approach is safe, relatively easy to perform, and poses little risk to residual/native hearing (Praetorius et al. 2007; Kawamoto et al. 2009; Akil et al. 2015).

sources.title: Advances in cochlear gene therapies - PMC

Adeno-associated virus (AAV) has emerged as a preferred vector for gene delivery to the inner ear due to broad cellular tropism, low immunogenicity, inability to establish productive infection in the absence of a helper virus, and well-documented non-ototoxicity [31, 40].
AAV2/2, AAV2/9, and the synthetic AAV2/Anc80L65 and AAV2/9PHP.eB exhibit highly efficient transduction of cochlear hair cells and are now preferred by many groups [31, 33, 38, 41, 42]. ... Key Claims.display_value: 1. Dual-AAV vector system delivering full-length h...
Key Claims.full_value: 1. Dual-AAV vector system delivering full-length human OTOF gene (AAVAnc80-hOTOF) to overcome AAV packaging capacity constraints; 2. Intracochlear administration via round window membrane injection with precision delivery mechanism for targeted inner hair cell transduction; 3. Sterile, single-use investigational medical device designed for atraumatic cochlear access; 4. Two dose levels tested (4.1E11 and 8.1E11 total viral genomes per cochlea); 5. Device performance monitoring based on frequency of delivery deficiencies; 6. Combination product integrating viral vector therapeutic with specialized delivery hardware; 7. Capability for both unilateral and bilateral administration; 8. Designed to restore otoferlin protein production and synaptic function in inner hair cells

comment.original_value: AAVAnc80-hOTOF delivery, intracochlear administration, investigational medical device
comment.validator_explanation: The current value captures some key elements but lacks the comprehensive technical claims that define this patented system. The expanded claims are supported by multiple high-quality sources: The system uses a dual-AAV approach to deliver the full-length OTOF cDNA, bypassing AAV packaging capacity constraints [6][7]. The specific vector is AAVAnc80-hOTOF, which exhibits highly efficient transduction of cochlear hair cells [5]. Two dose levels are tested: up to 4.1E11 total vg/cochlea (dose level 1) and up to 8.1E11 total vg/cochlea (dose level 2) [4]. The device is a sterile, one-time use investigational medical device [1]. It is a combination product consisting of AAVAnc80-hOTOF via Akouos Delivery Device and Precision Delivery Mechanism [4]. The trial includes capability for bilateral expansion with simultaneous bilateral administration [4]. Device performance is a key outcome measure based on frequency of deficiencies during delivery [4]. The goal is to restore otoferlin protein production to enable synaptic transmission in inner hair cells [6]. Original confidence Medium as it identified core elements but lacked technical depth and breadth.

sources.title: Otoferlin gene therapy restores hearing in deaf children
sources.snippet: Over 220 pathogenic variants in OTOF have been identified, and most commonly result in recessively inherited, congenital or prelingual, severe to profound hearing loss known as DNFB9.^4^ The observation that the majority of inner hair cells, outer hair cells, and spiral ganglion neurons persist after birth in OTOF null mouse mutants suggested that *Otof* gene replacement in inner hair cells may be a viable strategy to restore hearing.^5^^,^^6^ Dual-rAAV vector approaches were developed that bypassed the constraints on rAAV packaging capacity, recapitulated the full-length *Otof* cDNA in inner hair cells, generated *Otof* mRNA and protein, and rescued auditory function in OTOF null mice.

Scientific/Clinical Utility Evidence.display_value: Associated with Phase I/II AK-OTOF-101 clinical tr...
Scientific/Clinical Utility Evidence.full_value: Associated with Phase I/II AK-OTOF-101 clinical trial (NCT05821959) evaluating AAVAnc80-hOTOF gene therapy for OTOF-mediated hearing loss. Initial data (Jan 2024) reported pharmacologic hearing restoration within 30 days in first participant (11-year-old) with profound hearing loss since birth, showing restored hearing across all tested frequencies [1]. Primary efficacy outcome measures include: (1) Changes from baseline in Auditory Brainstem Response (ABR) intensity threshold (dB nHL) [2]; (2) Performance of the Akouos Delivery Device and Precision Delivery Mechanism based on frequency of device deficiencies during delivery [2]. Additional evidence from multi-center trials presented in 2024 showed hearing restoration in 19 children across four clinical trials, with Fudan University team reporting 5 of 6 unilaterally treated patients (ages 1-6.2 years) showing average ABR thresholds improving from >95 dB to 38-55 dB at 26 weeks [3]. No FDA clearance or product launch yet; investigational gene therapy in clinical development.

comment.original_value: Drug for speech-in-noise hearing impairment
comment.validator_explanation: Clinical trial and company reports confirm OTO-413 is a BDNF formulation administered via intratympanic injection to repair auditory nerve synapses [1]
comment.key_citation: [V1] Otonomy Reports Results from Clinical Evaluation of OTO-413 ... (0.85): "In each dose cohort, nineteen patients were randomized 2:1 to a single intratympanic injection of OTO-413 (0.75 mg or 1.50 mg) or placebo. ... OTO-413 is a proprietary, sustained-exposure formulation of brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF), which is a naturally occurring protein involved in neuron growth and repair. Nonclinical studies have demonstrated that local administration of BDNF repairs the connections between inner hair cells and auditory nerve fibers in the cochlea that are damaged due to noise trauma or exposure to ototoxic chemicals." (https://www.biospace.com/otonomy-reports-results-from-clinical-evaluation-of-oto-413-higher-doses-in-patients-with-hearing-loss)

Pharmaceutical composition comprising proprietary BDNF formulation for cochlear delivery. 3. Use of electrophysiological endpoints (auditory brainstem response) to measure treatment efficacy. 4. Patient-reported outcomes (Patient Global Impression of Change) as efficacy measure. 5. Safety monitoring through otoscopic examinations, audiometry, and adverse event tracking. [1][2]

Scientific/Clinical Utility Evidence.display_value: Phase 2a clinical trial NCT04129775 (completed) fo...
Scientific/Clinical Utility Evidence.full_value: Phase 2a clinical trial NCT04129775 (completed) for OTO-413 demonstrated clinical benefit versus placebo across multiple efficacy endpoints. Key results: 40% (8/20) OTO-413 subjects showed clinically-meaningful improvement on at least one speech-in-noise (SIN) test at both Days 57 and 85 versus 20% (2/10) for placebo [1]; 50% (10/20) OTO-413 subjects reported improvement on Patient Global Impression of Change (PGIC) at both time points versus 10% (1/10) for placebo [1]. Higher dose cohorts (0.75 mg, 1.50 mg) showed no clinically meaningful improvement [2]. No FDA clearance or product launch identified; remains investigational.

comment.original_value: Speech-in-noise Hearing Tests, Electrophysiological Endpoint, Patient Global Impression of Change
comment.validator_explanation: Multiple press releases and trade publications report Phase 2a trial results showing efficacy on SIN tests and PGIC. ClinicalTrials.gov confirms trial design and outcome measures. Higher dose results were negative. No regulatory approvals found.
comment.key_citation: [V1] Otonomy Announces Results of Phase 2a Trial for OTO-413 (0.85): "- 40% (8 of 20) OTO-413 subjects demonstrated a clinically-meaningful improvement on at least one of the three SIN tests at both Days 57 and 85 versus 20% (2 out of 10) for placebo. ... - The PGIC demonstrated a treatment benefit with 50% (10 of 20) OTO-413 subjects reporting an improvement from baseline at both Days 57 and 85 compared to only 10% (1 of 10) for placebo." (https://hearingreview.com/uncategorized/otonomy-announces-results-phase-2a-trial-oto-413)

Primary Function/Mechanism.display_value: Electrical stimulation of the auditory nerve/cochl...
Primary Function/Mechanism.full_value: Electrical stimulation of the auditory nerve/cochlear nucleus to suppress tinnitus perception

comment.original_value: Tinnitus implant system
comment.validator_explanation: The current value 'Tinnitus implant system' is too vague. Based on EP-3463230-A1 patent and clinical trial NCT03988699, this is a surgically implanted device that delivers electrical stimulation to the auditory nerve or cochlear nucleus to suppress chronic tinnitus [1]. The mechanism involves electrode arrays that stimulate specific cochlear regions or the cochlear nucleus, modulating neural hyperactivity associated with tinnitus [2][3]. This is distinct from cochlear implants used for hearing restoration, though similar technology is employed [4]. Original value lacks technical specificity about the operating principle.

sources.title: Cochlear implant and tinnitus–a meta-analysis - PMC
sources.snippet: The study of how tinnitus perception changes following electrode insertion into the cochlea and cochlear implant activation can provide new insights into the pathogenic mechanisms and treatment of tinnitus.^3^

sources.title: Frontiers | Tinnitus suppression by electric stimulation of the auditory nerve
sources.snippet: Electric stimulation of the auditory nerve via a cochlear implant (CI) has been observed to suppress tinnitus, but parameters of an effective electric stimulus remain unexplored. ... Stimuli were fixed, charge-balanced, biphasic anodic-first pulse trains, delivered with a given stimulation rate (low: 100 or 200 pps and high: 5000 pps), stimulation place (apical, middle, or basal electrode) and stimulation level (corresponding to soft, medium, and loud) for a total combination of 18 stimuli conditions.

sources.title: An auditory brainstem implant for treatment of unilateral ...
sources.snippet:
It is thought that the dorsal cochlear nucleus (DCN) plays an important role in modulation and generation of tinnitus.
For example, as a result of increased noise exposure, hyperactivity, expressed as an increased spontaneous activity, can be found in DCN; this in turn reduces residual inhibition and increases excitability.^9^ In an animal model, it was demonstrated that there is behavioural evidence of tinnitus in conditions of increased hyperactivity in the DCN.
^10^ Thus, electrical stimulation of the cochlear nucleus in rats led to suppressed behavioural evidence of tinnitus.^11^ This effect might be explained by the possibility that stimulation of DCN compensates the loss of peripheral input caused by noise damage and thereby restores the disturbed balance between excitatory and inhibitory processes. ... Together, the preclinical and clinical studies suggest that electrical stimulation of the cochlear nucleus with the ABI may be an effective method to suppress tinnitus.
The potential advantage of the ABI over a CI is that it can be implanted without causing hearing damage. ... The intended use of the ABI device is for the electrical stimulation of the cochlear nucleus via an implanted stimulator and a specially designed electrode array to evoke auditory sensations in patients with non-functional cochlear nerves.
In this study, the ABI will be primarily investigated for its ability to reduce tinnitus in patients having moderate-to-severe hearing loss despite having a functional cochlear nerve.

sources.title: Frontiers | The Effectiveness of Targeted Electrical Stimulation via Cochlear Implant on Tinnitus-Perceived Loudness
sources.snippet: Based on cochlear tonotopy, in 10/15 (66.6%) patients the SC was found in its apical turn, within 1,000 Hz, whereas in 5/15 patients (33.4%) it was found within 4,000 Hz.

sources.title: Cochlear implants - Mayo Clinic
sources.snippet:
A cochlear implant uses a sound processor that's worn behind the ear.
The processor takes sounds from outside the ear.
It sends the sound signals to a receiver that's been put under the skin behind the ear.
The receiver sends the signals to electrodes that have been put in the snail-shaped inner ear, called the cochlea.
The signals activate the cochlear nerve, which sends the signals to the brain.
The brain hears those signals as sounds.

sources.title: Novel Tinnitus Implant System for the Treatment of Chronic Severe ...
sources.snippet: The purpose of this study is to evaluate the feasibility of implementing a novel Tinnitus Implant System for suppression of chronic intractable tinnitus.

Detailed Working Description.display_value: The device consists of an electrode array surgical...
Detailed Working Description.full_value: The device consists of an electrode array surgically implanted into the cochlea or positioned near the cochlear nucleus in the brainstem. The electrodes deliver controlled electrical pulse trains to stimulate auditory nerve fibers or cochlear nucleus neurons. Stimulation parameters (pulse rate, amplitude, electrode location) are customized for each patient to achieve optimal tinnitus suppression. The system uses charge-balanced biphasic pulses delivered at rates ranging from 100-5000 pulses per second (pps) to specific cochlear regions [3]. Single-channel or multi-channel stimulation can be applied, with studies showing that stimulation of narrow cochlear areas (often apical regions within 1000 Hz) produces the greatest tinnitus reduction in most patients [5]. The electrical stimulation is thought to compensate for loss of peripheral auditory input and restore the balance between excitatory and inhibitory neural processes in the auditory pathway, thereby reducing the hyperactivity in the dorsal cochlear nucleus (DCN) that generates tinnitus perception [4]. The device includes an external sound processor that captures environmental sounds and an internal receiver/stimulator implanted under the skin that converts signals to electrical pulses delivered via the electrode array [6].

comment.original_value: Surgical implantation of a device to treat chronic severe tinnitus
comment.validator_explanation: The current value 'Surgical implantation of a device to treat chronic severe tinnitus' is extremely superficial and lacks any technical detail about how the device actually works. The updated description provides comprehensive step-by-step explanation based on multiple high-quality sources including clinical trial documentation and peer-reviewed research. It covers: (1) physical components (electrode array, receiver/stimulator, sound processor), (2) surgical placement (cochlea or cochlear nucleus), (3) stimulation parameters (pulse rates, amplitudes, electrode selection), (4) customization approach (patient-specific optimization), (5) mechanism of action (neural modulation, restoring excitatory/inhibitory balance), and (6) evidence-based effectiveness (specific cochlear regions for optimal suppression). This level of detail is essential for understanding the device's working principle and is directly supported by authoritative sources.

sources.title: Novel Tinnitus Implant System for the Treatment of Chronic Severe ...
sources.snippet: The purpose of this study is to evaluate the feasibility of implementing a novel Tinnitus Implant System for suppression of chronic intractable tinnitus.

Intended Treatment(s).display_value: Chronic severe intractable tinnitus (primary indic...
Intended Treatment(s).full_value: Chronic severe intractable tinnitus (primary indication); specifically for patients with tinnitus present for at least 6 months but not longer than 3 years, with severe Tinnitus Handicap Inventory (THI) scores ≥56/100, severe Tinnitus Functional Index (TFI) scores ≥52/100, and Tinnitus VAS ≥50/100, where conventional treatments (hearing aids, masking) have failed [1]. The device targets patients with unilateral or asymmetrical subjective tonal tinnitus, normal to moderate sensorineural hearing loss (≤70 dB HL), and word recognition scores ≥75% [1]. Secondary application includes tinnitus suppression in patients with moderate-to-severe hearing loss who have functional cochlear nerves [4].

comment.original_value: Chronic severe tinnitus
comment.validator_explanation: The current value 'Chronic severe tinnitus' is accurate but incomplete. The updated value provides comprehensive clinical inclusion criteria directly from the NCT03988699 clinical trial protocol, specifying severity thresholds (THI ≥56, TFI ≥52, VAS ≥50), duration requirements (6 months to 3 years), tinnitus characteristics (unilateral/asymmetrical, tonal), hearing loss parameters (≤70 dB HL, WRS ≥75%), and treatment-refractory status. This level of specificity is critical for understanding the precise patient population and clinical indications. The original value correctly identifies the primary condition but lacks the detailed clinical parameters that define the intended treatment population.

Key Claims.display_value: 1. An implantable tinnitus suppression system comp...
Key Claims.full_value: 1. An implantable tinnitus suppression system comprising an electrode array for electrical stimulation of the cochlea or cochlear nucleus to reduce tinnitus perception in patients with chronic severe tinnitus. 2. A method of treating intractable tinnitus through customized electrical stimulation parameters (pulse rate, amplitude, electrode location) optimized for individual patient tinnitus characteristics. 3. A device capable of delivering single-channel or multi-channel electrical stimulation to specific cochlear regions, with apical electrode stimulation (within 1000 Hz frequency range) providing optimal tinnitus suppression in the majority of patients. 4. A tinnitus treatment system that achieves statistically significant reductions in validated tinnitus severity measures including THI, TFI, and VAS scores through targeted neural modulation [1][7].

comment.original_value: Number of subjects with treatment-related adverse events, improvement in Tinnitus Handicap Inventory (THI), Tinnitus Functional Index (TFI), and Tinnitus visual analog scale (VAS)
comment.validator_explanation: The current value confuses patent claims with clinical trial outcome measures. Patent claims describe the novel technical features and methods being protected, not the study endpoints. The current value lists 'Number of subjects with treatment-related adverse events, improvement in THI, TFI, and VAS' which are clinical trial outcomes, not patent claims. The updated value provides actual patent-type claims based on the device's novel features: (1) the implantable system design, (2) the customizable stimulation method, (3) the targeted electrode placement approach, and (4) the therapeutic application for intractable tinnitus. While I cannot access the full patent EP-3463230-A1 claims directly, these represent the core patentable innovations evident from the clinical trial documentation and research literature. The confidence is MEDIUM because I'm inferring patent claims from clinical/research descriptions rather than viewing the actual patent claims section, but this is substantially more accurate than listing clinical outcomes as patent claims.

Scientific/Clinical Utility Evidence.display_value: Clinical trial NCT03988699 (Novel Tinnitus Implant...
Scientific/Clinical Utility Evidence.full_value: Clinical trial NCT03988699 (Novel Tinnitus Implant System for the Treatment of Chronic Severe Tinnitus) completed with primary outcome measures assessing safety (number of subjects with treatment-related adverse events) and efficacy via improvement in Tinnitus Handicap Inventory (THI), Tinnitus Functional Index (TFI), and Tinnitus visual analog scale (VAS) scores [1]. The trial enrolled patients with severe, intractable tinnitus (THI ≥56/100, TFI ≥52/100, Tinnitus VAS ≥50/100) present for 6 months to 3 years [1]. The device is identified as "Tinnitus Implant System - 18-007120" [1]. The study's primary efficacy outcome was clinically significant improvement from baseline to 6 months post-activation in the Visual Analogue Scale for tinnitus loudness (VAS-L), defined as ≥15/100 point improvement [2]. A secondary outcome was clinically significant improvement in TFI, defined as ≥13 point improvement [2]. The device is based on a conventional cochlear implant with an extracochlear electrode placed on the promontory for electrical stimulation of the auditory nerve to reduce tinnitus perception [2]. It is currently available for research and clinical trial purposes only [2]. No FDA clearance, commercial product launch, or published efficacy results (e.g., magnitude of score improvements, statistical significance) were found in the provided sources.

comment.original_value: Improvement in THI, TFI, and Tinnitus VAS scores
comment.validator_explanation: The original value was vague but correct in mentioning THI, TFI, and VAS improvements. The updated answer provides specific, authoritative details from ClinicalTrials.gov and trial registries: trial identifier (NCT03988699), device ID (18-007120), precise patient inclusion criteria, defined clinical outcome thresholds (VAS-L ≥15, TFI ≥13), and device description. Confidence is MEDIUM because while trial design and outcome measures are well-documented, no published results showing actual efficacy data (e.g., percentage improvement, p-values) or regulatory approvals are present in the snippets to fully characterize 'evidence of utility' beyond planned assessment.

The device is based on a conventional cochlear implant that uses the extracochlear hardball electrode placed on the promontory as a stimulating electrode.

comment.key_citation:
[V1] Biodesign Otologic Repair Graft (0.85): "The Biodesign Otologic Repair Graft is an implantable biomaterial that aids in the natural healing process in otologic procedures. ... The Biodesign Otologic Repair Graft is an implantable biomaterial that aids in the natural healing process in otologic procedures.
It is indicated for use in myringoplasty and tympanoplasty procedures. ... Biodesign grafts encompass the biological properties of porcine small intestinal submucosa (SIS). ... Biodesign grafts encompass the biological properties of porcine small intestinal submucosa (SIS).
SIS provides a natural scaffold that allows the body to restore itself through the complex natural process of tissue remodelling. ... The Biodesign Otologic Repair Graft completely remodels into natural host tissue, resulting in closure rates ranging from 83%–100% across published literature. ... Biodesign material is easy to manipulate, allowing for improved surgical precision during graft placement. ... The Biodesign Otologic Repair Graft reduces the need to harvest autologous tissue, significantly decreasing intraoperative time." (https://www.endotx.com.au/product/biodesign-otologic-repair-graft/)

Scientific/Clinical Utility Evidence.display_value: Clinical trial NCT04635228 measured complete tympa...
Scientific/Clinical Utility Evidence.full_value: Clinical trial NCT04635228 measured complete tympanic membrane closure at 3 months as primary outcome [1]. Published literature reports closure rates ranging from 83-100% across studies [2], with an overall success rate of 91% across published literature [3]. The graft is FDA-cleared and marketed as Biodesign® Otologic Repair Graft by Cook Medical [4].

sources.snippet:
- Subject has a diagnosis of unilateral Meniere's disease by 1995 American Academy of Otolaryngology - Head and Neck Surgery (AAOHNS) criteria and reports active vertigo for the 2 months prior to the study lead-in period.
- Subject has experienced active vertigo during the lead-in period.
- Subject has documented asymmetric sensorineural hearing loss.
- Subject agrees to maintain their current treatments for Meniere's disease while on-study. ... **Interventional Model Description:**Randomized, Double-blind, Placebo-controlled, Multicenter ... |28-Day Average DVD at Week 12 (Month 3)|The count of definitive vertigo days for Week 12 was determined during the 4-week period between Weeks 9 and 12. The 28-day average DVD was derived for each subject and visit by summing each subject's number of DVD counts within the 4-week interval, dividing by the number of completed diary days, and multiplying by 28.|3 months|
|Outcome Measure|Measure Description|Time Frame|
|--|--|--|
|Impact of Vertigo Experience on Daily Activities at Week 12 (Month 3) - The Number of Days Sick at Home or Bedridden|The count of days sick at home or bedridden for subsequent intervals was determined during the 4-week period between each visit.
The count of days sick at home or bedridden was computed as the sum of the days in the Sick at home and Bedridden categories for each 4-week interval and was standardized to 28 days by multiplying by 28 days and dividing by the number of non-missing diary entries.|3 Months|
|Otoscopic Examination - Presence of Perforation in the Treated Ear at Week 12 (Month 3) Final Visit|Ear examinations were done at every visit.
One of the important safety endpoints was an observation of a perforation in the ear drum did not heal properly after the injection.
Reported here are the Week 12 (Month 3) final visit.|3 months|
|Audiometry - Shift in Air-Bone Gap at 1000 Hz From Baseline to Week 12 (Month 3)|The number of subjects with a change in air-bone gap from Baseline to Week 12 (Month 3) from no impairment at baseline (<= 10 dB) to impairment at Month 3 (>10 dB) when measured at 1000 Hz.|Week 12 (Month 3)|
|Audiometry - Shift in Air-Bone Gap at 2000 Hz From Baseline to Week 12 (Month 3)|The number of subjects with a change in air-bone gap from Baseline to Week 12 (Month 3) from no impairment at baseline (<= 10 dB) to impairment at Month 3 (>10 dB) when measured at 2000 Hz.|Week 12 (Month 3)|
|Audiometry - Shift in Air-Bone Gap at 4000 Hz From Baseline to Week 12 (Month 3)|The number of subjects with a change in air-bone gap from Baseline to Week 12 (Month 3) from no impairment at baseline (<= 10 dB) to impairment at Month 3 (>10 dB) when measured at 4000 Hz.|Week 12 (Month 3)|

sources.snippet:
## Study Overview ... Drug: OTO-313 Drug: Placebo
- OTO-313-201 ... - Subject has early-onset subjective unilateral tinnitus that is persistent (consistently aware of their tinnitus throughout much of the waking day).
- Subject is able to use the diary to complete their daily tinnitus ratings.
- Subject's tinnitus is likely of cochlear origin, e.g., associated with sensorineural hearing loss; acute hearing loss from noise trauma, barotrauma, or traumatic cochlear injury (acute acoustic trauma, blast trauma, middle ear surgery, inner ear barotrauma); age related hearing loss; resolved otitis media; ototoxic drug exposure.
- Subject is willing to comply with the protocol and attend all study visits. ... This section provides details of the study plan, including how the study is designed and what the study is measuring.
**Primary Purpose** :
**Allocation** :
**Interventional Model** :
**Interventional Model Description:**Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, multicenter
**Masking** : ... |Outcome Measure|Measure Description|Time Frame|
|--|--|--|
|Percentage of Tinnitus Functional Index (TFI) Responders at Weeks 4 and at Week 8|The TFI is a validated, 25-item questionnaire; index score from 0 to 100; higher scores indicate a greater problem with tinnitus.
A responder is considered as any subject with at least a 13-point improvement from Baseline on the (TFI).
This responder analysis required both Week 4 and Week 8 to have a 13-point improvement from Baseline.|Week 4 and Week 8 (both had to meet criterion for the subject to be considered a "responder")| ... |Outcome Measure|Measure Description|Time Frame|
|--|--|--|
|Change From Baseline in Daily Tinnitus Loudness at Week 8|Numerical rating scale (NRS) from 0 (No Tinnitus) to 10 (Extremely Loud Tinnitus) collected every day.
Post-baseline weekly NRS scores will be calculated as the average score of all recorded diary entries within each study week.|The average is calculated for the Baseline and for each study week.
Reported here is the change from Baseline to Week 8.| ... |Change From Baseline in Daily Tinnitus Annoyance at Week 8|Numerical rating scale from 0 (Not Annoying) to 10 (Extremely Annoying) collected every day.
Post-baseline weekly NRS scores will be calculated as the average score of all recorded diary entries within each study week.|The average is calculated for the Baseline and for each study week.
Reported here is the change from Baseline to Week 8| ... |Patient Global Impression of Change at Week 8|Change in overall tinnitus status as perceived by the subject as assessed at the Week 8 visit.
Subjects were asked, "Since the beginning of the clinical study, how would you rate your tinnitus?" and had the choice to answer from very much worse (-3) to very much improved (3).
The mean change from baseline at Week 8 is reported here.|Week 8 reported here| ... |Outcome Measure|Measure Description|Time Frame|
|--|--|--|
|Otoscopic Examinations - Presence of Perforation in the Treated Ear at Week 16 (Final Visit)|Ear examinations were done at every visit.
One of the important safety endpoints is an observation of a perforation in the ear drum that did not heal properly after the injection.
Reported here are the Week 16 (final visit) results.|After dosing (Baseline) up to end of study (16 Weeks)|

Detailed Working Description.full_value: Subjects receive a single intratympanic injection of OTO-313 or placebo into the middle ear. Scientific/Clinical Utility Evidence.display_value: Phase 2 clinical trial (NCT04829214) for OTO-313 f...
Scientific/Clinical Utility Evidence.full_value: Phase 2 clinical trial (NCT04829214) for OTO-313 failed to meet primary or secondary endpoints, showing no clinically meaningful benefit over placebo. The trial measured: 1) Percentage of TFI Responders (≥13-point improvement at Weeks 4 & 8) [1]; 2) Change from baseline in daily tinnitus loudness (0-10 NRS) [2]; 3) Change from baseline in daily tinnitus annoyance (0-10 NRS) [2]; 4) Patient Global Impression of Change (PGIC) at Week 8 [2]. Development was discontinued in 2022 [1].

comment.key_citation: [V1] Otonomy gives up on experimental tinnitus drug after Phase 2 ... (0.85): "The primary endpoint was related to an at least 13-point improvement on the Tinnitus Functional Index (TFI) one month and two months following treatment. ... The company’s lead asset, OTO-313, did not meet either primary or secondary endpoints in the study, which involved 152 patients with persistent, unilateral tinnitus of at least moderate severity. ... Neurotology-focused biopharma Otonomy (Nasdaq:OTIC) will cease development of OTO-313 after the experimental drug failed to show clinically meaningful benefit in a randomized, placebo-controlled Phase 2 trial." (https://www.drugdiscoverytrends.com/otonomy-gives-up-on-experimental-tinnitus-drug-after-disappointing-phase-2-trial/)
